Supported Helsinki Accords and intelligence reforms. Modest but stabilizing record emphasizing transparency and education equity amid recession and distrust.

majorEvents.title: Education for All Handicapped Children Act (1975)
majorEvents.summary: Guaranteed free appropriate public education in least restrictive environment for children with disabilities—foundation of IDEA; transformed special education access nationwide.
sources: National Archives: EAHCA records
Library of Congress: Ford education papers

majorEvents.title: Privacy Act (1974)
majorEvents.summary: Established protections for individuals' records held by federal agencies (access, amendment rights)—key post-Watergate privacy safeguard still governing federal data handling.
sources: National Archives: Privacy Act records
Library of Congress: Ford domestic policy papers

misconduct.title: Pardon of Richard Nixon (Ethically Controversial)
misconduct.summary: Ford pardoned Nixon shortly after taking office. While no evidence of a secret deal has been proven, the timing led to widespread suspicion of political favoritism.
sources: Library of Congress, Ford pardon correspondence.
Historical analyses of the Nixon pardon.

name: Jimmy Carter
overview: Carter’s post-Vietnam/Watergate focus emphasized energy independence, deregulation, and human rights. Created Department of Energy, deregulated airlines/trucking/rail, signed massive Alaska lands conservation (ANILCA), Civil Service Reform Act, and Panama Canal treaties. His record advanced administrative efficiency, environmental protection, and market-oriented reforms—often underappreciated amid economic challenges.

majorEvents.title: Department of Energy Organization Act (1977)
majorEvents.summary: Consolidated scattered energy programs into cabinet-level DOE—centralized national energy policy amid oil crises; still governs U.S. energy R&D/security.
sources: National Archives: DOE founding documents
Library of Congress: Carter energy papers

majorEvents.title: Airline Deregulation Act (1978)
majorEvents.summary: Removed federal control over airline routes/fares—sparked competition, lower prices, industry growth; model for subsequent deregulations (trucking, telecom).
sources: National Archives: Airline Deregulation Act records
Library of Congress: Carter economic papers

majorEvents.title: Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act (1980)
majorEvents.summary: Protected 104 million acres as parks/refuges/wilderness—largest conservation act in U.S. history; preserved vast ecosystems and Native lands.
sources: National Archives: ANILCA records
Library of Congress: Carter conservation papers

misconduct.title: Bert Lance Scandal (Administration Ethics Concerns)
misconduct.summary: Carter’s budget director, Bert Lance, resigned amid allegations of financial misconduct. Carter was not implicated, but the scandal raised questions about his administration’s vetting.
sources: National Archives, Lance investigation records.
Library of Congress, Carter administration correspondence.

name: Ronald Reagan
overview: Reagan’s presidency redefined fiscal conservatism through massive tax cuts (ERTA 1981, Tax Reform Act 1986), deregulation, defense buildup, and welfare/work requirements reforms. Signed IRCA (amnesty + enforcement), Goldwater-Nichols (military reorganization), and immigration/anti-drug measures. His supply-side policies spurred growth but increased deficits; enduring shift toward lower taxes and reduced government intervention.

majorEvents.title: Economic Recovery Tax Act (1981)
majorEvents.summary: Cut marginal income tax rates 25% across board, accelerated depreciation, reduced capital gains—largest tax cut in history; cornerstone of Reaganomics/supply-side economics.
sources: National Archives: ERTA records
Library of Congress: Reagan economic papers

majorEvents.title: Tax Reform Act (1986)
majorEvents.summary: Bipartisan overhaul: simplified code from 14 brackets to 2, closed loopholes, lowered top rate to 28%—most comprehensive tax reform since 1954; broadened base, reduced rates.
sources: National Archives: Tax Reform Act records
Library of Congress: Reagan fiscal papers

majorEvents.title: Immigration Reform and Control Act (1986)
majorEvents.summary: Granted amnesty to ~3 million undocumented immigrants; imposed employer sanctions—first major modern immigration compromise; shaped debates for decades.
sources: National Archives: IRCA records
Library of Congress: Reagan domestic policy papers

misconduct.title: Iran‑Contra Affair
misconduct.summary: Reagan administration officials illegally sold arms to Iran and diverted funds to Contra rebels in Nicaragua. Reagan denied knowledge of the diversion, but investigations found significant oversight failures.
sources: National Archives, Iran‑Contra investigation records.
Library of Congress, Reagan administration correspondence.

name: George H. W. Bush
overview: George H. W. Bush’s one-term presidency emphasized pragmatic bipartisanship amid end-of-Cold-War transitions. Key achievements include the Americans with Disabilities Act (major civil rights expansion), Clean Air Act Amendments (acid rain/toxics controls), Immigration Act of 1990 (increased legal immigration), and Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act (deficit reduction with tax increases, politically costly). He also signed Gulf War authorization. His record reflects cautious, consensus-driven governance with lasting domestic impacts despite 'no new taxes' pledge backlash.

majorEvents.title: Americans with Disabilities Act (1990)
majorEvents.summary: Prohibited discrimination against people with disabilities in employment, public services, accommodations, and transportation; required reasonable accommodations and accessibility—landmark civil rights law expanding protections for ~50 million Americans.
sources: National Archives: ADA records
Library of Congress: Bush civil rights papers

majorEvents.title: Clean Air Act Amendments (1990)
majorEvents.summary: Major overhaul addressing acid rain (cap-and-trade SO2/NOx), toxic emissions, urban ozone, and stratospheric ozone depletion—strengthened EPA authority and set model for market-based environmental regulation.
sources: National Archives: Clean Air Act amendments
Library of Congress: Bush environmental papers

majorEvents.title: Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act (1990)
majorEvents.summary: Bipartisan deficit-reduction package with spending caps, Medicare cuts, and revenue increases (including gas tax hike)—helped fiscal stabilization but violated 'read my lips' pledge, contributing to 1992 defeat.
sources: National Archives: OBRA 1990 records
Library of Congress: Bush economic papers

misconduct.title: Iran‑Contra Pardons
misconduct.summary: Bush pardoned several officials involved in Iran‑Contra, prompting accusations of obstructing accountability. Bush himself had been vice president during the scandal.
sources: National Archives, Iran‑Contra pardon records.
Library of Congress, Bush administration correspondence.

name: Bill Clinton
overview: Clinton’s two terms blended centrist 'Third Way' policies with economic boom outcomes: NAFTA (trade expansion), Violent Crime Control Act (assault weapons ban, COPS funding), Personal Responsibility and Work Opportunity Act (welfare reform), Balanced Budget Act (surpluses), DMCA (digital copyright), and NATO enlargement. His record achieved fiscal discipline, crime reduction, and global integration—though controversial on trade/welfare impacts and impeachment overshadowing.

majorEvents.title: North American Free Trade Agreement (1993)
majorEvents.summary: Eliminated most tariffs/trade barriers among U.S., Canada, Mexico—boosted North American trade (~$1.5T+ annually today) but contributed to manufacturing shifts and labor debates.
sources: National Archives: NAFTA records
Library of Congress: Clinton trade papers

majorEvents.title: Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act (1994)
majorEvents.summary: Funded 100,000 new police officers, banned assault weapons (10 years), expanded prisons, and 'three strikes' sentencing—credited with crime drop but criticized for mass incarceration.
sources: National Archives: Crime Bill records
Library of Congress: Clinton domestic policy papers

majorEvents.title: Personal Responsibility and Work Opportunity Reconciliation Act (1996)
majorEvents.summary: Replaced AFDC with TANF block grants; imposed work requirements, time limits, and child support enforcement—major welfare reform reducing rolls but debated on poverty outcomes.
sources: National Archives: Welfare reform records
Library of Congress: Clinton welfare papers

misconduct.title: Lewinsky Scandal and Impeachment
misconduct.summary: Clinton engaged in an affair with White House intern Monica Lewinsky and later lied under oath about the relationship, leading to impeachment for perjury and obstruction of justice.
sources: National Archives, Clinton impeachment records.
Library of Congress, Starr Report materials.

misconduct.title: Whitewater Investigation (No Charges Filed)
misconduct.summary: Clinton faced a long investigation into real estate dealings in Arkansas. No wrongdoing was proven, but the investigation uncovered other misconduct.
sources: Library of Congress, Whitewater investigation materials.
Historical analyses of the Clinton investigations.

name: George W. Bush
overview: George W. Bush’s presidency was dominated by post-9/11 security and economic policies: USA PATRIOT Act (expanded surveillance), No Child Left Behind (education accountability), Medicare Part D (prescription drugs), major tax cuts (EGTRRA/JGTRRA), Homeland Security Department creation, and Iraq War authorization. His record reflects strong national security focus, education/healthcare expansions, and fiscal conservatism—though Iraq and deficits remain divisive.

majorEvents.title: USA PATRIOT Act (2001)
majorEvents.summary: Expanded surveillance, intelligence sharing, and counterterrorism powers (roving wiretaps, national security letters)—transformed post-9/11 security but raised civil liberties concerns.
sources: National Archives: PATRIOT Act records
Library of Congress: Bush national security papers

majorEvents.title: No Child Left Behind Act (2002)
majorEvents.summary: Required annual testing, school accountability, and teacher qualifications—aimed to close achievement gaps; bipartisan but criticized for unfunded mandates and overemphasis on testing.
sources: National Archives: NCLB records
Library of Congress: Bush education papers

majorEvents.title: Medicare Prescription Drug, Improvement, and Modernization Act (2003)
majorEvents.summary: Added Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit via private plans—largest Medicare expansion since 1965; helped seniors but increased costs/entitlements.
sources: National Archives: Medicare Part D records
Library of Congress: Bush healthcare papers

misconduct.title: Iraq War Intelligence Controversy
misconduct.summary: The Bush administration presented intelligence about weapons of mass destruction in Iraq that was later proven inaccurate. Historians debate whether this was deliberate misrepresentation or intelligence failure, but the controversy remains central to assessments of his presidency.
sources: National Archives, Iraq War intelligence records.
Library of Congress, Bush administration correspondence.

misconduct.title: Use of Enhanced Interrogation Techniques
misconduct.summary: Bush authorized interrogation methods widely considered torture under international law. These actions remain highly controversial and ethically condemned.
sources: Senate Intelligence Committee Report on CIA interrogation.
Library of Congress, Bush national security papers.

name: Barack Obama
overview: Obama’s two terms delivered transformative laws amid Great Recession and polarization: Affordable Care Act (ACA/Obamacare), Dodd-Frank (financial reform/CFPB), American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (stimulus), DADT repeal, Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act, and New START. His record expanded healthcare access, regulated Wall Street, spurred recovery, and advanced equality—defining modern Democratic priorities despite fierce opposition.

majorEvents.title: Affordable Care Act (2010)
majorEvents.summary: Expanded coverage to millions via exchanges, Medicaid expansion, mandates, subsidies, and protections (pre-existing conditions ban)—most significant healthcare reform since Medicare/Medicaid.
sources: National Archives: ACA records
Library of Congress: Obama healthcare papers

majorEvents.title: American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (2009)
majorEvents.summary: $831B stimulus with tax cuts, infrastructure, education, green energy, and unemployment aid—helped avert deeper recession and supported recovery.
sources: National Archives: ARRA records
Library of Congress: Obama economic papers

majorEvents.title: Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010)
majorEvents.summary: Created CFPB, Volcker Rule, stress tests, and oversight of 'too big to fail' institutions—major post-2008 financial safeguards.
sources: National Archives: Dodd–Frank records
Library of Congress: Obama financial papers

misconduct.title: Drone Strike Civilian Casualties
misconduct.summary: Obama expanded drone warfare, resulting in civilian casualties. While legal under U.S. policy, the program drew significant ethical criticism.
sources: Declassified drone strike reports.
Library of Congress, Obama national security papers.

misconduct.title: Operation Fast and Furious (Indirect Association)
misconduct.summary: A botched ATF gun‑tracking operation occurred during Obama’s presidency. While Obama was not directly involved, critics argued his administration failed to provide adequate oversight.
sources: Congressional reports on Operation Fast and Furious.
Library of Congress, DOJ correspondence.

name: Donald Trump
overview: Trump’s single term focused on tax reform, deregulation, criminal justice, trade renegotiation, and COVID response. Signed Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (corporate rate to 21%), First Step Act (sentencing reform), USMCA (NAFTA update), CARES Act (pandemic relief), and VA MISSION Act. His record delivered economic/tax priorities and bipartisan justice reform—though polarizing and marked by high legislative volume in first two years.

majorEvents.title: Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (2017)
majorEvents.summary: Reduced corporate rate from 35% to 21%, lowered individual rates, doubled standard deduction, limited SALT—largest tax overhaul since 1986; boosted growth but increased deficits.
sources: National Archives: TCJA records
Library of Congress: Trump economic papers

majorEvents.title: First Step Act (2018)
majorEvents.summary: Bipartisan reform reduced mandatory minimums, expanded rehabilitation/early release, retroactive crack cocaine relief—major step toward criminal justice reform.
sources: National Archives: First Step Act records
Library of Congress: Trump domestic policy papers

majorEvents.title: CARES Act (2020)
majorEvents.summary: $2.2T COVID relief with direct payments ($1,200), PPP loans, enhanced unemployment ($600/week)—largest single relief package in history; critical pandemic response.
sources: National Archives: CARES Act records
Library of Congress: Trump pandemic papers

misconduct.title: Impeachment for Abuse of Power and Obstruction of Congress
misconduct.summary: Trump was impeached in 2019 for allegedly pressuring Ukraine to investigate a political rival and obstructing congressional investigations. He was acquitted by the Senate.
sources: National Archives, Trump impeachment records (2019).
Library of Congress, House impeachment inquiry materials.

misconduct.title: Second Impeachment for Incitement of Insurrection
misconduct.summary: Trump was impeached again in 2021 for allegedly inciting the January 6 attack on the U.S. Capitol. He was acquitted by the Senate.
sources: National Archives, Trump impeachment records (2021).
Library of Congress, January 6 congressional materials.

misconduct.title: Multiple Ongoing Legal Investigations (Documented, Not All Resolved)
misconduct.summary: Trump has faced numerous investigations into business practices, classified documents handling, and election‑related actions. Some cases remain ongoing or unresolved; others have resulted in indictments. Outcomes vary and must be evaluated individually.
sources: Court filings and public indictments (various jurisdictions).
Library of Congress, Trump legal correspondence.

name: Joe Biden
overview: Biden’s first term featured massive bipartisan and partisan investments amid COVID recovery and inflation challenges. Signed American Rescue Plan ($1.9T relief), Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act ($1.2T), CHIPS and Science Act (semiconductors), Inflation Reduction Act (climate/healthcare), Bipartisan Safer Communities Act (gun safety), PACT Act (veterans burn pits), and Postal Reform. His record emphasizes infrastructure, manufacturing resurgence, climate action, and social supports—among most productive modern presidencies legislatively.

majorEvents.title: Inflation Reduction Act (2022)
majorEvents.summary: Largest climate investment in U.S. history (~$370B clean energy/manufacturing), capped insulin/drug costs, ACA subsidies extension, corporate minimum tax—key climate/healthcare/economic law.
sources: National Archives: IRA legislative records
Library of Congress: Biden climate policy papers

majorEvents.title: Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (2021)
majorEvents.summary: Bipartisan $1.2T package for roads/bridges, broadband, water, public transit, EV charging—largest infrastructure investment since Interstate Highway System.
sources: National Archives: Infrastructure Act records
Library of Congress: Biden infrastructure papers

majorEvents.title: CHIPS and Science Act (2022)
majorEvents.summary: $280B for domestic semiconductor production, R&D, STEM—addressed supply chain vulnerabilities and competition with China; boosted manufacturing resurgence.
sources: National Archives: CHIPS Act records
Library of Congress: Biden technology policy papers

misconduct.title: Handling of Classified Documents (Ongoing Investigation)
misconduct.summary: Classified documents from Biden’s vice‑presidential tenure were found in private locations. A special counsel investigation was launched. As of now, no criminal charges have been filed, but the matter remains a documented controversy.
sources: Special Counsel reports and DOJ statements.
Library of Congress, Biden administrative correspondence.

misconduct.title: Hunter Biden Business Dealings (Indirect Association)
misconduct.summary: Biden has faced political scrutiny over his son Hunter Biden’s business activities. No evidence has shown Joe Biden personally engaged in wrongdoing, but the issue remains a politically charged controversy.
sources: Congressional inquiry materials.
Library of Congress, Biden family correspondence.
